responses: Gillette, 20 years, 20 years.
60 years old

I have been representing the hard working people of Gillette for three terms.  I’m a Christian and veteran naval officer. I married my college sweetheart Sage. We raised two children in Gillette and our daughter lives here with her family.  My family and friends are the primary reason I wish to serve the people of Campbell County.

Today the families in House District 31 are facing inflation that is cutting into their budget everyday.  I have advanced real and lasting tax relief for the people of Wyoming and I will continue to do that as well as striving to ensure that state and local governments do not add to the cost of achieving the American dream.

The One Big Beautiful Bill will provide nearly $1 billion to Wyoming for Rural Health Transformation.  I authored the bill determining how this money will be spent.  Wyoming must take advantage of this unprecidented opportunity and expend these funds wisely to increase access and the sutainability of our healthcare industry.

I have no interest in selling public lands.  If Wyoming residents want to have the public lands managed for their best interests, then the government closest to the people of Wyoming should be the government to manage those lands.  The recent Rock Springs Resource Management Plan(RMP) proved that the people in New York and California have no business dictating how our lands are utilized.  That federal control will lead to zero human access if we continue to allow the federal government to prioritize so called environmentalism above hunting, fishing, camping, and mineral utilization.  I believe the state should take all lands that are public and that our constitution should require that public access be maintained at or above current levels.

I would ensure, as I already have through my work as chairman of the House Appropriations Committee, that local governments have access to steady streams of revenue other than residential property taxes.  I led the way in redistributing sales tax, already being collected for the state, to local governments.  This is exactly what good conservative governance looks like.

The state should first and foremost create a business freindly environment by having the lowest tax  burden and the least restrictive regulatory burden.  The Wyoming Business Counsel should be the chief advertising and promoting arm of the state when it comes to developing new business relationships.  The infrastructure needed to support new economic devleopment should be streamlined so that local governents can effect change as quickly as they desire to see new businesses come to their communities.

I believe that the current system honors both transparency for those who wish to see who is funding whom and the first amendment rights of individuals who wish to participate in this representative government.

Gillette 48 yrs. Born in Michigan

Live in Gillette 48 of my 55 years. I’ve worked in the coal industry for 30 yrs. 20 of that in management responsible for large work force and large budgets. Served in this community in various volunteer roles and have owned several businesses in the past and currently have a business that my wife manages.

Biggest challenges/opportunities in Wyo is responsible economic development. A lot of big projects, data centers processing plants want to build in Wyo. I am for diversification but it needs to make economic sense and protections need to be in place for our communities.
There is only one health care insurance provider in Wyoming. We need to work at getting more options for the people of Wyoming. Free market competition should  lower prices.
No. Public needs to stay public. We need to make sure the federal and state agencies in Wyoming are held accountable and make sure they are staffed properly to do the job.

We need to revisit the SF69 of 2025 that targeted a reduction of 50% on the increase between 2020-2024 which was a time period of very in inflation on home Assessments. The blanket approach does work. Voters in Wy don’t only want but desire strategic and permanent tax relief while still protecting essential services.
Providing attractive infrastructure. Set up regulations and policies that hold companies accountable.
I think it is adequate.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
